frames.caption: dp = new int[6] zero-fills the table; dp[0] = 0 (zero has no 1-bits). Now sweep i = 1..5 left to right.
frames.note: dp[0] = 0 is the base case: zero has no set bits. new int[n+1] zero-fills the whole table, so dp[0] is already correct before the loop even starts. Every later entry will be derived from an entry to its LEFT, which is why a single left-to-right sweep is enough — no dependency is ever read before it is written.

frames.caption: i = 1 (001): read dp[i>>1] = dp[0] = 0 (popcount of 1 with its low bit dropped).
frames.note: The trick: i >> 1 drops i's lowest bit, giving a strictly smaller number whose popcount we ALREADY computed earlier in this same sweep. So dp[i >> 1] is a free lookup, not a recomputation — that reuse is the entire reason this is O(n) instead of O(n log n).

frames.caption: dp[1] = dp[0] + (1 & 1) = 0 + 1 = 1.
frames.note: i & 1 isolates exactly the bit we dropped with i >> 1. If that bit was a 1 we must add it back (popcount of i = popcount of i>>1 + 1); if it was 0 we add nothing. dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1) does both cases at once in O(1).

frames.caption: i = 2 (010): read dp[i>>1] = dp[1] = 1 (popcount of 2 with its low bit dropped).
frames.note: The trick: i >> 1 drops i's lowest bit, giving a strictly smaller number whose popcount we ALREADY computed earlier in this same sweep. So dp[i >> 1] is a free lookup, not a recomputation — that reuse is the entire reason this is O(n) instead of O(n log n).

frames.caption: dp[2] = dp[1] + (2 & 1) = 1 + 0 = 1.
frames.note: i & 1 isolates exactly the bit we dropped with i >> 1. If that bit was a 1 we must add it back (popcount of i = popcount of i>>1 + 1); if it was 0 we add nothing. dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1) does both cases at once in O(1).

frames.caption: i = 3 (011): read dp[i>>1] = dp[1] = 1 (popcount of 3 with its low bit dropped).
frames.note: The trick: i >> 1 drops i's lowest bit, giving a strictly smaller number whose popcount we ALREADY computed earlier in this same sweep. So dp[i >> 1] is a free lookup, not a recomputation — that reuse is the entire reason this is O(n) instead of O(n log n).

frames.caption: dp[3] = dp[1] + (3 & 1) = 1 + 1 = 2.
frames.note: i & 1 isolates exactly the bit we dropped with i >> 1. If that bit was a 1 we must add it back (popcount of i = popcount of i>>1 + 1); if it was 0 we add nothing. dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1) does both cases at once in O(1).

frames.caption: i = 4 (100): read dp[i>>1] = dp[2] = 1 (popcount of 4 with its low bit dropped).
frames.note: The trick: i >> 1 drops i's lowest bit, giving a strictly smaller number whose popcount we ALREADY computed earlier in this same sweep. So dp[i >> 1] is a free lookup, not a recomputation — that reuse is the entire reason this is O(n) instead of O(n log n).

frames.caption: dp[4] = dp[2] + (4 & 1) = 1 + 0 = 1.
frames.note: i & 1 isolates exactly the bit we dropped with i >> 1. If that bit was a 1 we must add it back (popcount of i = popcount of i>>1 + 1); if it was 0 we add nothing. dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1) does both cases at once in O(1).

frames.caption: i = 5 (101): read dp[i>>1] = dp[2] = 1 (popcount of 5 with its low bit dropped).
frames.note: The trick: i >> 1 drops i's lowest bit, giving a strictly smaller number whose popcount we ALREADY computed earlier in this same sweep. So dp[i >> 1] is a free lookup, not a recomputation — that reuse is the entire reason this is O(n) instead of O(n log n).

frames.caption: dp[5] = dp[2] + (5 & 1) = 1 + 1 = 2.
frames.note: i & 1 isolates exactly the bit we dropped with i >> 1. If that bit was a 1 we must add it back (popcount of i = popcount of i>>1 + 1); if it was 0 we add nothing. dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1) does both cases at once in O(1).

frames.caption: i > 5: loop exits. Return dp = [0, 1, 1, 2, 1, 2].
frames.note: Every entry cost O(1) given the entries to its left, and we filled n of them, so the whole array is O(n) time. The O(n) space is the output array itself — required by the problem, not avoidable scratch. Each number literally reused the popcount of half of itself.
